name: Maneuvering Jet Overhaul
description: Aftermarket attitude thrusters and upgraded reaction control systems. The ship rolls faster, pitches harder, and recovers from maneuvers with less overshoot. When the Pilot uses Evasive Maneuvers and succeeds, the [Elusive] condition also grants [Cover 1] to the ship for the same duration.
designNotes: Entry-level Handling upgrade. Makes Evasive Maneuvers strictly better by bundling Cover with Elusive. Rewards defensive flying without changing the action's structure.

name: Snap Roll
description: On Evasive Maneuvers, pay -1 tier. The ship executes a violent axial roll that shakes all [Locked On] conditions targeting this ship. The roll is disorienting enough that any ship that had [Locked On] on this ship has their next Dogfight action [Weakened].
designNotes: Defensive gambit. Breaks locks without needing to win a Dogfight disengage — the Pilot sheds pursuit through sheer violence of maneuver. The -1 tier means the Elusive effect is weaker, but the lock-shatter compensates tactically.

name: Brace for Impact
description: On Angle Deflector Shields, pay -1 tier. The Pilot physically angles the ship to present the strongest shield facet to incoming fire while the Operator manages the deflector output — coordinated defense between two stations. On a successful Angle Deflector Shields, the Effect Tier is treated as +1 higher than rolled (net zero with the gambit cost at base, but +1 above the shield floor on failure). The Pilot's flying makes the Operator's shields more effective. 'Brace for impact' — the Pilot calls it, the ship turns, the shields catch it.
designNotes: Cross-station gambit. The Pilot contributes to the Operator's shield defense by angling the ship. The -1 tier gambit cost is offset by the +1 tier bonus on success, making it net-neutral at base but creating a higher ceiling when the Operator rolls well. At Masterful (reduced to Fleeting by cost, then boosted back to Masterful by the bonus), the defense is the same — but at Legendary (reduced to Masterful, boosted to Legendary), the full effect fires. The real value is the +1 on the floor — even on failure, the Pilot's angling adds to the shield's minimum absorption.

name: Vector Flip
description: On Dogfight (when choosing the disengage option), pay -1 tier. Instead of moving 1 zone away, the ship executes a 180-degree flip and remains in the zone — now facing the opponent head-on at point-blank range. All Forward-arc weapons may fire. The opponent is [Exposed] until end of round. You chose to break the lock, but instead of running you turned and bared your teeth.
designNotes: Aggressive gambit on the Dogfight disengage option. Turns a disengagement into a counterattack opportunity. The opponent expected you to run — instead you're staring them down with guns hot. The -1 tier means the Dogfight disengage effect itself is weaker (you don't get the full tier benefit), but the tactical reversal compensates.

name: The Corellian Wake
description: The ship's maneuvering systems are tuned to an almost supernatural level of precision. When the Pilot rolls a Control result of 8+ on any Handling-powered action (Evasive Maneuvers, Terrain Run, Evade), the standard +1 Effect Tier bonus is doubled to +2 Effect Tiers. In hazardous environments (asteroid fields, debris clouds, atmospheric hazards), the ship moves through obstacles that should be impassable — threading gaps that no sane pilot would attempt. The Corellian Wake is pure flying mastery — no gimmicks, no tricks, just the best hands on the stick in the sector.
designNotes: D12 Handling option A. Pure flying enhancement — doubles the 8+ bonus on all Handling actions. The Pilot who picks this is betting on skill, not hyperspace shortcuts. Exclusive with Micro-Jump (Handling) — pick one.

name: Micro-Jump (Handling)
description: The ship's navigation computer and maneuvering systems are integrated to a degree that allows short-range hyperspace skips — the ship jumps a fraction of a light-second, repositioning instantaneously within the combat zone. Uses Piloting (Control) / Handling (Power). The Handling version is precision — the Pilot threads the exit point with surgical accuracy. Failure is a navigation abort: the calculations collapse, the ship goes nowhere, and the Pilot is left [Exposed].

microJump.failureFlavor: Navigation abort — the jump calculations collapse. The Pilot yanks the lever back. Ship goes nowhere, [Exposed] for the round.

effect.label: Failure
effect.description: Navigation abort. The jump calculations collapse mid-sequence. Ship goes nowhere. [Exposed] for the round.

effect.label: Fleeting
effect.description: Violent skip. Jump to any zone, shatter all [Locked On]. Handling or Engines Impaired (Pilot's choice) from the stress.

effect.label: Masterful
effect.description: Clean jump. Reposition to any zone, shatter locks, establish [Locked On] on an enemy of choice.

effect.label: Legendary
effect.description: Perfect jump. Reposition, shatter locks, establish [Locked On], all Gunners gain [Optimized] (Tactical).

designNotes: D12 Handling option B. Unlocks Micro-Jump using Handling as Power die — precision exit with nav-abort failure. Exclusive with Corellian Wake — pick one. The same Micro-Jump maneuver is available via D12 Engines (Class Zero Hyperdrive), but with Engines as Power and engine-sputter failure.

name: Polarized Deflectors
description: Shield emitters are recalibrated to polarize against the most common energy weapon frequencies. When the ship uses Angle Deflector Shields as its defense against energy weapons (lasers, turbolasers), the Shields Power die is [Optimized] for that roll. Does not apply against ordnance (torpedoes, missiles) or kinetic impacts.
designNotes: Entry-level Shields upgrade. Makes Angle Deflector Shields more reliable against the most common attack type (energy weapons). Simple, consistent benefit that rewards choosing Angle Deflector Shields over Evade against blasters.

name: Energy Shunt
description: On Reinforce Shields, pay -1 tier. The shield energy that would have created a buffer is instead shunted into a friendly ship at Close range, granting them [Buffered 1]. The Engineer's own ship does not gain a buffer — the energy is redirected entirely. At Masterful (reduced to Fleeting by gambit cost), the friendly ship gains [Buffered 1] for 1 round. At Legendary (reduced to Masterful), they gain [Buffered 2] for the fight.
designNotes: Support gambit. Lets the Engineer protect another ship instead of their own. Useful in escort scenarios or when flying alongside a more vulnerable ally. The -1 tier cost means the buffer granted is always one step weaker than a self-reinforce.

name: Regenerative Cycling
description: Shield generators cycle through a regenerative feedback loop that restores depleted shield layers between hits. At the start of each round, if the ship has no [Buffered] condition, it automatically gains [Buffered 1]. This does not stack — if the ship already has [Buffered], the regeneration does not apply. The shields passively rebuild a minimal layer of protection every round without the Engineer spending an action.
designNotes: Passive shield regeneration. Guarantees a minimum shield buffer every round without action cost. The non-stacking rule prevents it from being abused with Reinforce Shields — if you already have a buffer, regen doesn't add to it. This frees the Engineer to Jury Rig or Reroute instead of constantly Reinforcing.

name: Backscatter Flare
description: On Angle Deflector Shields, pay -1 tier. The shield absorption releases a violent backscatter pulse toward the attacker. The attacker's next action this round has its Control die [Weakened]. The flare is blinding — concentrated shield energy reflected back along the attack vector. The -1 tier reduces the damage absorption, but the tactical disruption to the attacker compensates.
designNotes: Defensive gambit that punishes the attacker. Turns Angle Deflector Shields into a counterattack opportunity — the attacker lands the hit, but the shield flare degrades their next action. Good against enemies who focus fire on one target.

name: Impenetrable Baffles
description: Layered baffle plating and redundant shield generators create an almost impervious defensive envelope. When Reinforce Shields succeeds, the [Buffered X] value is increased by 1 above what the tier would normally grant. Fleeting grants [Buffered 2] instead of [Buffered 1]. Masterful grants [Buffered 3] instead of [Buffered 2]. Legendary grants [Buffered 4] instead of [Buffered 3]. The ship simply absorbs more punishment per reinforcement cycle.
designNotes: D12 Shields passive. Strictly improves Reinforce Shields output. The extra buffer point per tier makes the Engineer's defensive actions significantly more efficient. Combined with Regenerative Cycling (D10), the ship becomes extremely hard to damage through sustained fire.

name: Aegis Protocol
description: Advanced shield networking allows the ship to extend its shield envelope to protect adjacent allied ships. When this ship has [Buffered X], allied ships at Close range can use this ship's [Buffered] value to absorb damage instead of their own defenses. The protecting ship's buffer decreases as normal when absorbing damage for an ally. Only one ship can benefit from the Aegis per attack — the Engineer chooses which ally to protect when the attack is declared.
designNotes: D12 Shields alternative passive. Turns the ship into a mobile shield platform — a tank that protects its allies. Pairs naturally with Impenetrable Baffles (same gate, can't have both) to create two distinct D12 Shield identities: the self-sufficient fortress (Baffles) or the fleet protector (Aegis).

name: Overtuned Sublight Drive
description: Aftermarket injectors and upgraded thrust couplings push more raw output through the drive. Any maneuver that moves the ship X zones increases X by 1. Fly moves one zone further. Attack Run closes one extra zone. Dogfight disengage moves one extra zone. The ship is simply faster — a Corellian racing shop upgrade.
designNotes: Entry-level Engines upgrade. Simple, consistent benefit — more zones moved per action. Makes the Pilot's positioning actions strictly better without changing their structure.

name: Power Bleed
description: On Reroute Power, pay -1 tier. Engines steps down as normal (source system), but the rerouted power feeds two target systems instead of one — both step up per the resolved tier. The -1 tier affects duration: a Masterful result drops to Fleeting — two systems boosted but only for 1 round instead of fight-long. One system long-term, or two systems short-term.
designNotes: Utility gambit. Doubles the targets of Reroute Power at the cost of reduced tier. Excellent for emergency situations where multiple systems need a boost simultaneously.

name: Field-Serviceable Drive
description: Modular engine housing with accessible maintenance panels and standardized components — military-grade serviceability designed for combat repairs. When the Engineer attempts to Jury Rig the Engines system during combat, their Control die is [Optimized]. The engines are built to be fixed under fire. Does not change what Jury Rig does — just makes it more likely to succeed and more likely to hit 8+ for the bonus tier shift.
designNotes: Passive that improves Jury Rig specifically for Engines. Rewards the Engineer for maintaining the engine — the modular design means repairs are faster and more reliable. Pairs well with the D8 Overtuned Sublight (the racing engine that breaks more often, but is easier to fix).

name: Strafing Thrusters
description: On Attack Run, pay -1 tier. After the Attack Run resolves, the ship repositions 1 zone in any direction (including laterally) as a free movement. Close in, fire, keep moving. The Gunner gets a reduced-tier shot, but the ship is not sitting in front of the enemy's guns when the attack is over.
designNotes: Aggressive mobility gambit. Turns Attack Run into a hit-and-run — close, shoot, reposition. The -1 tier means the Gunner buffs are weaker, but the repositioning prevents the ship from being a stationary target after committing to the attack.

name: Class Zero Hyperdrive
description: The hyperdrive motivator is modified to a classification that should not exist for this ship's size class. Imperial Star Destroyers run Class 2. Military interceptors run Class 1. This ship runs Class 0.5 or better. Between encounters, the ship reaches any known destination in half the expected travel time. Cannot be caught in hyperspace — nothing is faster. This passive also unlocks the Micro-Jump maneuver using Piloting (Control) / Engines (Power). The Engines version is raw power — the oversized drive punches through to hyperspace by brute force. Failure is mechanical: the hyperdrive sputters and chokes, the ship goes nowhere, [Exposed] for the round, and Engines steps down from the failed ignition.

microJump.failureFlavor: Engine sputter — the hyperdrive coughs and chokes, refusing to engage. The Pilot is slamming the console. Ship goes nowhere, [Exposed] for the round. Engines steps down from the failed ignition sequence.

effect.label: Failure
effect.description: The hyperdrive sputters and chokes — will not engage. Ship goes nowhere, [Exposed] for the round. Engines steps down from the failed ignition sequence.

effect.label: Fleeting
effect.description: Violent skip. Jump to any zone, shatter all [Locked On]. Engines or Handling Impaired (Pilot's choice) from the stress.

effect.label: Masterful
effect.description: Clean jump. Reposition to any zone, shatter locks, establish [Locked On] on an enemy of choice.

effect.label: Legendary
effect.description: Perfect jump. Reposition, shatter locks, establish [Locked On], all Gunners gain [Optimized] (Tactical).

designNotes: D12 Engines option A. Bundles 0.5 hyperdrive travel speed + Micro-Jump access using Engines as Power die. Failure is mechanical — the drive will not fire — and costs an Engine step-down on top of [Exposed]. The Falcon moment: Chewie howling, Han punching the dashboard. Exclusive with Oversized Reactor Core — pick one.

name: Oversized Reactor Core
description: The engine is massively over-spec for the hull — a power plant designed for a ship two size classes larger. When the crew chooses to impair Engines to soak damage, the Engines system soaks Hull Rating + 2 additional damage instead of the standard Hull Rating value. On a Hull 2 ship, most systems soak 2 per impairment — Engines soaks 4. On a Hull 1 fighter, most systems soak 1 — Engines soaks 3. The engine is so overbuilt that damage has to chew through layers of redundant machinery before it actually affects output. The crew has a tactical reason to direct damage into Engines whenever possible — it is the most efficient soak target on the ship.
designNotes: D12 Engines alternative passive. Turns Engines into the ship's armor — an oversized power plant that absorbs punishment like a capital ship. Two very different D12 Engine identities: Class Zero (the ship that cannot be caught, micro-jump escape) vs Oversized Reactor (the ship that refuses to die, engines absorb hits). Speed vs durability. The smuggler vs the blockade runner.

name: Targeting Uplink
description: Upgraded sensor-to-weapons integration — the targeting computer feeds real-time tracking data directly to the gun mounts. When the Gunner makes an Attack or Called Shot, the Gunner's Control die gains [Optimized] when attacking a target that the Operator has successfully Scanned this fight. The X-Wing's targeting computer — military-grade sensors feeding precise firing solutions to the weapons. Without this mod, gunners aim on instinct and visual. With it, they have a computer painting the target for them.
designNotes: D8 Sensors option A. Rewards Operator-Gunner coordination — the Operator scans, the Gunner shoots better. The benefit requires the Operator to have done their job (successful Scan) before the Gunner benefits. Makes Scan matter in combat without bolting mechanical effects onto the Scan maneuver itself.

name: Diagnostic Network
description: Internal sensor feeds integrated across all stations — every console now has access to the ship's sensor suite. All stations gain access to the Scan maneuver (normally Operator-only). The Pilot can scan for terrain hazards, the Gunner can scan for weak points, the Engineer can scan for system diagnostics. Each station uses their own Control discipline but the Power die is always Sensors. Any Scan gambits not specifically restricted to a single station are available to all stations as well.
designNotes: D8 Sensors option B. Spreads sensor capability across the whole crew. Dramatically increases crew flexibility — any station can spend their action to gather information. Makes the ship smarter through shared data, not individual power. Creates tactical choices: the Gunner could attack, or they could Scan to set up a better attack next round. Two different D8 sensor philosophies: Targeting Uplink (focused precision, Operator-Gunner partnership) vs Diagnostic Network (shared awareness, whole-crew benefit).

name: Signal Masking Array
description: Upgraded emitter baffling and signal management software that lets the ship actively suppress its electronic signature while running at reduced power. The ship can run silent during narrative play — reducing power output, masking emissions with subspace noise, and minimizing its sensor footprint. When the crew is trying to avoid detection (slipping past a patrol, hiding in an asteroid field, approaching a station unnoticed), the Operator can use Scan to actively manage the ship's signature. The Sensors Power die is used as the ship's stealth rating for any opposed checks against enemy detection. Does not function in active starship combat — once detected and engaged, the masking is broken. Hiding a starship from active line-of-sight sensors in a dogfight is not realistic.
designNotes: D10 Sensors option A. Narrative stealth capability — the Ghost, the Falcon clinging to the Star Destroyer's hull, a smuggler's ship drifting through a checkpoint. Gives the ship the ability to sneak, which most ships cannot do. Purely narrative — no combat stealth mechanics. The Operator uses Sensors as a stealth rating, which means upgrading sensors also upgrades stealth. Two different D10 sensor identities: Signal Masking (the ship that disappears outside combat) vs Sensor Prioritization (the Operator commanding the ship's electronic posture in combat).

name: Sensor Prioritization
description: Advanced sensor processing allows the Operator to dynamically allocate the ship's sensor bandwidth between offensive and defensive functions — but not both simultaneously. The Operator declares one of two modes as a maneuver: Offensive Priority ('All power to targeting') — all attacks from the ship (Gunner attacks, Pilot attack actions, Jam) are [Empowered], but all defensive reactions (Evade, Countermeasures, Angle Deflector Shields) cannot benefit from any sensor-related buffs or gambits. Defensive Priority ('All power to shields and countermeasures') — all defensive reactions (Evade, Countermeasures, Angle Deflector Shields) are [Empowered], but all attacks from the ship cannot benefit from any sensor-related buffs or gambits. The mode persists until the Operator spends another maneuver to switch it, or until they drop it entirely to return to balanced operation.
designNotes: D10 Sensors option B. Tactical mode-switching that gives the Operator command over the ship's entire electronic posture. Creates a genuine decision every round — the tactical situation changes and the Operator must decide where sensor bandwidth goes. Very Star Wars: 'Angle the deflector shields!' is literally this. A D10 sensor suite has enough processing power to prioritize but not enough to fully cover both offense and defense simultaneously.

name: Crystal Gravfield Trap
description: A gravitic sensor array so sensitive it detects mass disturbances at a system-wide scale. Originally designed for capital ships hunting cloaked vessels, this miniaturized version gives a freighter-class ship awareness that rivals a Star Destroyer's com-scan department. The Operator's Scan maneuver gains the following enhancements: Scan has no practical range limit — the Operator can scan targets anywhere in the system, not just in the immediate combat zone. Scan detects things that are normally invisible to sensors — ships running silent, powered-down vessels, objects hiding in debris fields or asteroid belts, even ships on hyperspace approach (the gravitic disturbance of their mass shadow is detectable seconds before arrival). When the Operator's Control roll on a Scan hits 8+, the standard +1 Effect Tier bonus is doubled to +2 Effect Tiers. Nothing is hidden from this ship.
designNotes: D12 Sensors option A. The all-seeing eye — system-wide awareness, detecting the undetectable, information supremacy. Crystal gravfield traps are canon Imperial technology used on Interdictor cruisers and intelligence vessels since before 32 BBY. This mod miniaturizes that capability. The doubled 8+ bonus mirrors the Corellian Wake (Handling D12) design — rewarding the Operator's skill with exponentially better results. Exclusive with Feedback Cascade — pick one. The intelligence vessel vs the electronic warfare predator.

name: Feedback Cascade
description: The sensor suite is weaponized. The Operator captures the target's active sensor emissions, inverts them, and dumps the corrupted data back through the target's own sensor dish — creating a feedback loop that overwhelms their processing systems from the inside out. This is not hacking or slicing — it is brute-force electronic destruction, the sensor equivalent of pointing a microphone at its own speaker.

feedbackCascade.prerequisite: Target must currently be [Jammed] (the Operator has already mapped their sensor frequencies)

effect.label: Fleeting
effect.description: Target's Sensors gain Impaired (Tactical). The feedback hits but they are managing the overload — cycling filters, rerouting processing. A debilitating blow, but contained.

effect.label: Masterful
effect.description: Duration increases to Lingering. If the target's Sensors are already Impaired by this maneuver, increase the impairment by one step (Impaired becomes Debilitated, Debilitated becomes Disabled). The cascade is eating through their countermeasures.

effect.label: Legendary
effect.description: Target's Sensors are instantly Disabled. The system crashes catastrophically — receivers burned out, processing cores fused. Additionally, the cascading failure leaves the ship and crew [Rattled]. The electronic scream reverberates through every console on the ship.

feedbackCascade.failure: No effect. Action spent. The target's countermeasures absorbed the feedback before it could cascade. The Operator cannot attempt Feedback Cascade against this target again this encounter — the target has identified and adapted to the attack pattern.

designNotes: D12 Sensors option B. Electronic warfare capstone — direct sensor system damage through a feedback loop. Requires [Jammed] prerequisite, making it a two-action combo: Jam first to map frequencies, then Cascade to burn them out. Fleeting imposes Impaired (Tactical), Masterful escalates to Lingering and stacks impairment, Legendary is the kill shot — instant Disabled plus [Rattled] across the crew. On failure, cannot reattempt against the same target — the enemy adapts. Exclusive with Crystal Gravfield Trap — pick one. One finds the needle in the haystack, the other sets the haystack on fire.
